metric_value: Oracle Apps World 2002 messaging shifted from new-product launch to 'let's get you guys to use what we already have' — focused on upgrading the installed base to 11i.
notes: Author interpretation consistent with keynote content.

metric_value: Approximately 50% of Oracle client base (~1,500 customers) still on a 10x release of the applications suite.
notes: Specific figure supplied by Oracle at Apps World.

metric_value: Initial releases of Oracle 11i had quality assurance (QA) problems that further dampened upgrade enthusiasm among existing customers.
Oracle added 'daily close' capability to 11i, enabling virtual closing for any part of the organization on a daily cadence. Lawson Software users had this feature first.
Virtual close supports shorter period-end closing cycles, more timely financial reporting (internal and external), and improved decision-making.
Oracle renewed its 'we'll do it for you' outsourcing pledge to users, even offering to buy back customer investment in hardware and IT staff to support the application suite.
Oracle claimed outsourcing to Oracle can save customers 31% to 84% on IT infrastructure costs (vendor-cited ranges).
Oracle claimed product and service response time can improve by as much as 50% when Oracle manages and maintains its own software.
Senior executive Mark Barranchea moved from heading CRM to spearhead a newly created top-level customer-care and relations role.
Oracle Apps World marked a rapprochement with the Oracle Application User Group (OAUG), an independent users association, after years of tension.
Larry Ellison's tone at Apps World was markedly more conciliatory toward partners and customers than in prior years ('A Kinder, Gentler Larry Ellison?').
notes: Reflected in partner ASP endorsements vs. prior 'only Oracle hosts Oracle' stance.

metric_value: Aberdeen expects customers to respond positively to many components of Oracle's service and hosting propositions.
notes: Proved correct — Oracle On Demand → Oracle Cloud became major revenue line.
Oracle Cloud Applications became multi-billion-dollar business; SaaS dominated enterprise apps market.

metric_value: Aberdeen predicts businesses won't be rushed to upgrade due to economy, IT investment slowdown, and magnitude of effort involved.
Customer loyalty and support helps companies weather crises (witness Baan); can only help Oracle as well.
notes: Baan was itself sold to Invensys in 2000 after customer exodus.

metric_value: Upgrades are key 'unlocking' events for incremental revenue opportunities within an installed base; newest version is the platform for extended functionality.
Across all enterprise environments, the pace of keeping current with revision releases is declining (2002).
